agent1_snippets.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Therapies
agent1_snippets.content: Gene therapies include medications that bind RNA and skip over the defective codon. This produces a shorter but potentially functional protein. Eteplirsen is an exon 51 skipping antisense oligonucleotide medication used for this purpose. Eteplirsen has been approved by the FDA for this purpose.
agent1_snippets.query_used:
Is eteplirsen effective for the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy?

Options:
A: yes
B: no

agent1_snippets.title: Rhabdomyolysis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
agent1_snippets.content: Gene therapy is being explored for treating inherited muscular dystrophies such as Duchenne and Becker. This includes various technologies such as read-through of early stop codons and exon skipping. Edasalonexent (CAT-1004), a nuclear factor κB (NF-κB) inhibitor that combines structural elements of salicylic acid and docosahexaenoic acid, has been evaluated in a phase 3, placebo-controlled clinical trial with some reported success. ( NCT03703882 ). Vamorolone is a glucocorticoid that stabilizes membranes by reducing lipid peroxidation and inhibiting NF-kB-mediated inflammation; it has also demonstrated functional improvement of symptoms. Many other novel treatments are being studied. [20] [64]

agent1_snippets.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmaceutical Interventions
agent1_snippets.content: Golodirsen (SRP-4053), an antisense therapy designed to treat Duchenne muscular dystrophy, is approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). However, the evidence supporting its use, particularly in cases requiring exon 53 skipping, is still evolving and not fully established. [197] [198]

agent1_snippets.title: Dystrophinopathies -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
agent1_snippets.content: To date, there are several therapies under investigation, from gene repair and therapy to drugs affecting the expression of dystrophin. Eteplirsen is one of the latter. Approved by the FDA for infusion in 2016, it works by skipping exon 51 during pre-mRNA splicing correcting dystrophin expression. [21] Ataluren is another drug on trials, with the goal of bypassing pathogenic variants through promoting ribosomal read-through, therefore continuing dystrophin expression. [22]

agent1_snippets.content: Previous clinical trials have shown no clear benefit from metformin treatment with or without L-citrulline. agent1_snippets.title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Therapy of Muscle Dystrophy
agent1_snippets.content: There is no approved BMD treatment to date. However, the management approach to this condition is often similar to that for milder DMD cases. Corticosteroids have been the mainstay of treatment for patients with significant muscle weakness. agent1_snippets.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: No medical cure exists for this congenital dystrophy, and the disease has a poor prognosis. Treatment is centered on glucocorticoid therapy, prevention of contractures, and medical care of cardiomyopathy and respiratory compromise. [10] [11]

agent1_snippets.title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Therapy of Muscle Dystrophy
agent1_snippets.content: Corticosteroids should be started before physical disability develops and must continue even after losing ambulation. Glucocorticoid treatment helps prolong survival by delaying the onset of cardiomyopathy, improving pulmonary function, retarding scoliosis, and reducing the need for spinal surgery. [28] [29] The steroid dose should be reduced by 25% to 33% if adverse effects develop. Nitric oxide is occasionally prescribed to improve muscular circulation.

B: no

agent1_snippets.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmaceutical Interventions
agent1_snippets.content: Steroids, specifically glucocorticoids like prednisone, administered at a dose of 0.75 mg/kg per day, significantly slow the progression of muscular dystrophy for up to 3 years. [189] While some patients may not tolerate glucocorticoid therapy due to factors like weight gain and an increased risk of fractures, recent evidence suggests that early oral steroid use can lead to markedly improved outcomes. [190] [191] This includes additional walking years for children and increased life expectancy. [71] The use of glucocorticoids in Becker dystrophy and their effectiveness in treating myotonic muscular dystrophy remain areas where research is ongoing, and evidence is not yet conclusive. [192] [193] Some individuals with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy improve with steroid therapy, particularly when rapidly progressive weakness is observed. [194]

agent1_snippets.content: Glucocorticoid therapy decreases the rate of apoptosis of myotubes and can decelerate myofiber necrosis. Prednisone is recommended at a dosage of (0.75 mg/kg per day or 10 mg/kg per week is given over two weekend days).

agent1_snippets.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Cardiomyopathy
agent1_snippets.content: Surveillance consists of a cardiology assessment with ECG and echocardiogram. This should be performed at the time of diagnosis or by the age of 6 years. Routine surveillance should be performed once every two years until the age of 10 and then yearly after that. If evidence of cardiomyopathy is present, surveillance every six months is indicated.

agent1_snippets.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Other Interventions
agent1_snippets.content: Genetic counseling is recommended, especially for X-linked inheritance cases. [208] Male siblings have a 50% chance of being affected, while female siblings have a 50% chance of being carriers. If the affected individual has children, all daughters will be carriers of this X-linked recessive disorder. Genetic counseling should be extended to the mother, female siblings, offspring, and maternal relatives.

agent1_snippets.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Risdiplam - This drug is an oral medication that works via modifying SMN2 splicing and thereby increasing functional SMN protein levels. There have been numerous phase II/III trials that have shown efficacy with regards to improvement in motor function for patients with type II and III. Clinical trials are still ongoing and are currently under review in the US, Europe, and the UK for licensing. [38]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1016
agent2_snippets.content: E [Preparations] is a drug class that includes enalapril, efedrin, eprozinol and eteplirsen as its direct members, and also serves as the parent category for efinaconazole. Enalapril is an angiotensin‑converting‑enzyme inhibitor, efedrin is a sympathomimetic agent, efinaconazole is an antifungal preparation, eprozinol functions both as a member of the E [Preparations] group and as an H1‑histamine antagonist, and eteplirsen is an antisense oligonucleotide used for Duchenne muscular dystrophy. The H1‑histamine antagonist class (antagonisté histaminu H1) encompasses the specific agents triprolidin, eprozinol and protopine, indicating that these compounds share the pharmacologic property of blocking H1 histamine receptors. Additionally, both aceneuramic acid and eteplirsen are classified under the ATC category “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” linking them to therapeutic use in musculoskeletal conditions. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
agent2_snippets.content: MUSCULO‑SKELETAL SYSTEM DRUGS serve as the overarching class for two major ATC subcategories: “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system” and “topical products for joint and muscular pain.” Both subcategories are therefore considered specific types of musculo‑skeletal system drugs. Within the topical products subcategory, capsaicin and similar agents are classified as members of the product group zucapsaicin and are explicitly identified as a type of topical product for joint and muscular pain. The broader group “Other topical products for joint and muscular pain in ATC” is itself a subclass of “topical products for joint and muscular pain,” and tolazolin is listed as a member of this group. The “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system” category includes several agents that are not primarily topical. Palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen, and nusinersen are all members of this category. Nusinersen, although primarily known for its use in spinal muscular atrophy, is also linked to the physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis; mupirocin shares this same physiologic effect. Additionally, mupirocin is classified under “Other nasal preparations in ATC,” indicating its use in a nasal formulation. Overall, the knowledge base delineates a hierarchical organization of musculo‑skeletal system drugs, distinguishing between topical pain‑relief products (such as capsaicin‑based formulations and tolazolin) and systemic agents for musculo‑skeletal disorders (including palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen, nusinersen, and mupirocin), while also highlighting shared pharmacologic actions like the promotion of increased protein synthesis.
